item.title: Re: What exactly does this thing do?
item.content:encoded: May I suggest you read this canon?

item.title: What exactly does this thing do?
item.content:encoded: It sounds like there nothing strange about it at all since the conditions required to activate it will kill the subject and that's it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So apparently this is part of the end of death canon which i have yet to read, but this shit is hilarious on its own.
+1

\o/ \o/ \o/ \o/

Thanks to everyone who read this! The fact that this thing is still chugging along almost a year later is sort of astounding to me. Here's to more End of Death! which will hopefully be coming soon ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I was reading Season One of the ΩK Canon, I did start to wonder how the event impacted day-to-day Foundation research, especially considering how many D-class are supposedly terminated by either testing or the Foundation outside of the EODS.
What really impressed me is that, unlike most mainlisters, being a TTKY doesn't detract from it, since being able to be a TTKY is what makes it anomalous in the first place. Really intriguing start to Season Two!

Pardon while I try not to think about all the former D-class trapped alive in decaying bodies by the Foundation post-ΩK.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't really like the idea. ΩK implies immortality, and yet there is still a way to die. Wonder if there are other anomalies who are able to kill despite ΩK.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not really feeling the article even after learning of its context, so no vote for now.

However, I'd like to point out this misplaced modifier:

Foundation personnel were alerted after the local paper published a story covering the event, who administered amnestics to all witnessing parties.

It almost seems like the local paper gave amnestics to witnesses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm just going to imagine that the note was superceded by somebody and it gets used liberally.

Yeahhh…that's better…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent article. At first i thought it was a joke, and was going to downvote it, but after learning about the end of death canon, i love it. You earned an upvote from me good sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well to answer the question, you're indeed wrong. There are plenty of SCPs based on canons1, Doomcon for example.

You can have your opinion, but the reasoning behind the downvote are kind of baseless.

Footnotes
1. not cannons, those are the military equipment

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely not, no. You have a fundamental misunderstanding of how the site works.

EDIT: To expand on that; this site is for writing, worldbuilding, and storytelling. For many years, SCP articles have formed parts of series or canons — hell, even the Antimemetics Division series, quantitatively the most popular on the site (going by scpper.db at least), has SCP-3125 as a component. To add to that, the contest this canon was written for was based around telling a narrative through tales, GoI formats, and yes, SCPs.

It's perfectly okay to prefer SCP articles that tell standalone narratives, and it's okay not to be interested in the site beyond the mainlist — despite that, it is also okay (and in some circumstances, very much encouraged) to do what Kirby was doing here. I usually try to judge works on their content rather than their context, anyway — you might find it useful if you did the same :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm probably wrong on this, but aren't the tales and cannons supposed to be based off of the mainsite, instead of the other way around?

Either way, I personally think that they should be separate entities, so it's a -1 from me.

EDIT: You bring up some good points, I personally really love Antimemetics Division and the Resurrection Canon, and there are SCPs based on the various cannons. I suppose it just confuses me little when canons that are separate from the main series are part of the main canon. So, I take back my downvote. I apologize if what I said was rude, I'm still kinda new to the ins and outs of the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do wonder if the Ethics Committee would keep the knife in play for cases where the subject is too far gone to put in a robo suit(A pile of brains in a bag in the river for example) So a quick thrust with the knife to just put them out of their misery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it's somewhat baffling and a complete blindside if you're not already familiar with omega-K, but is also a nice introduction to the canon… the weirdness builds gradually but at a nice pace, e.g. with the age (and then seniority) of the test subjects climbing ever higher…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bugs the crap out of me he melted off a chunk of the knife to use as a bullet. Especially such a large bullet like a 45. I mean, still an upvote but what a dumb move by the dude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First pass: holy shit, TTKU went mainline?
After reading: okay, someone is definitely pulling a cultural appropriation (!?) of TTKU into the mainline and, in fact, into a legitimately disturbing canon. Upvoting this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. I think this SCP is one of the ones most in line with the spirit of the original, SCP 173- it's simple and concise but it implies something much more encompassing and horrifying. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would argue that this actually has a deeper story behind it than just being a premise. I would argue that, beyond the reveal, this acts to characterize Young, who is carrying out the experiments. There's also the implied narrative that comes with the fact that they start killing Foundation Personnel as part of testing.

Now, if you have no interest in the canon, or haven't read the rest of the canon, then this probably has no meaning to you, and that's ok. I wrote this SCP as part of the end of death canon, and a lot of the underlying narrative relies heavily upon it, so downvoting it because it doesn't stand on its own is entirely reasonable.

But I guess what I'm trying to say is, this is far more than a premise. I'm not saying that you're unjustified in not recognizing that since it's built on background material, but there's definitely a story being told here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvote from me. I think any article that is heavily reliant on another work, is a part of a canon or series of some kind, or uses major gimmicks; should use those things as a spice, not as a life-support. That is to say, it should be good enough to stand on its own, and then use those other things to enhance itself from that point on.

Now, in this case, it's very hard to separate the skip's effect from its connection to the canon. But after thinking about it some, I don't think this one would quite survive on its own. A closest analogue, I think, would be a knife in the mainline canon that is making people immortal. And that I would downvote in an instant, because it's just a premise, and the article fails to do anything with it.

Similarly here, the fact that it can kill people is the premise, and then it is not build upon or used in an interesting way. The great reveal is that the events occur inside the No Death canon, and that's a very poor reveal to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought it was a joke SCP, untill I saw this was End-of-Death.
Well executed.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For SCP-3154 it's less that they don't know what death is, and more that the shard of glass is an infohazard, causing anybody who witnesses death caused by the shard to not put together than the shard has caused death, and so they feel that they have to explain the phenomenon in their usual clinical way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh. Executed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i was on my way to downvoting this until i saw it was part of doomsday

and i saw the author too.

"oh shit thats what it is"

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't that exactly what 3154 does, except that in 3154 world they don't even understand what death is?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
spoilers
haters will see me and be beset by locusts

This knife kills people in a world where people normally can't die, therefore making it anomalous by that world's standards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ಠ_ಠ was .. was he just stabbing people all day .. is its power that it makes you wont to get knifed .. did he trick people .. the world will never know

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Legitimately thought that it was just a fairly average knife and the anomalous property trigger list was just assorted ways to die. Then I got to the addendum.

Now I have a new hub to read (: Clever stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Woah, had to check for a -J halfway through, definitely got me at the end!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only works for the End of the Death Hub.

And because of that, +1. I hope there are more tales based off of this as it seems like a glitch in the matrix given the implications of death.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That twist hit me like a truck. Well played.

item.title: +100 :D
item.content:encoded:
Thank you all for reading! Now I don't have to return the tombstones saying "Died for 100 upvotes" :P

Stay tuned for the next episode! Hopefully I'll get it out in a week or two, depending on how hectic real life gets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yet another long explanation of my probably peculiar and one-off headcanon
– hide block


I would disagree, but mostly because of the headcanon I'm using. If the Foundation is not reestablishing the old numbers, then that would most likely fall under their a category of "deciding what reality is considered normal", which is not their job. Their job is to make sure reality maintains a certain baseline, not to adjust the baseline.

Now I understand that, what is considered "normal" is definitely subject and relative and WJS's proposal explore's a possible way in which the Foundation is making the call on what qualifies all the time. However, the way that I tend to write the Foundation, they do not actively try to change baseline reality to something arbitrary. Reality should conform to how it is "naturally".

Therefore, in EODS there have been two "natural" states with respect to death counts. The Pre-EODS state, and the Post-EODS state. Either there is an attempt to restore the world to the Pre-EODS state, which would mean that the numbers would have to return, or keep the world in the current, Post-EODS state which is a death count of zero. Anything in between or different would qualify to them as an arbitrary, artificial normalcy, and is against the organizations MO.

Now is this somewhat hypocritical? Yes. However I believe this is hypocritical stance is once the Foundation would take if confronted with the idea that they are "just bending reality to whatever fits them best", because that is not their goal. At least not how I write them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Using this one thing to reestablish the old death count numbers would be a nightmare, and most likely unfeasible.

That's hardly an argument for not using it at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you enjoyed!

As for for the shard of glass, that's SCP-3154, which was probably one of my favorite things to come out of jamcon.

I thought this was a joke article without the J in the title

I've gotten this a lot, that people initially think its a -J. Part of me understands why (because its sort of makes fun of the "thing that kills you" trope), but the other part of me is surprised this is considered funny. Like, I know when I wrote it I was hoping it'd come off as somewhat absurd, but I never quite saw it as humorous. Whatever works, works I guess!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hear a of couple people here talking about a "Scantron glass shard" here, can someone please tell me the number for it?
Oh and its a good SCP, although due to me just hitting the top rated SCP of the month as of now and not knowing this is an End of death SCP at first, I thought this was a joke article without the J in the title.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i was like what and then i read the bottom and reread and saw the ages and i was like OHHHHHH and then i upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get it. And now the whole article makes sense. Well done, Kirby. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's correct!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, to be sure I'm understanding this right, this knife is anomalous because the End Of Death scenario from the Doomsday Contest is still in play?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ok this was a longer explanation than I expected
– hide block

While I agree that is the excerpt from the hub, one thing that I will note is that the excerpt has been written from a pre-omega-k point of view (it comes from a big book they have on all k-class scenarios).

However, as part of season 1, as the Foundation has to deal with Omega-K, they go about redefining normalcy so they operate within it. Since they don’t know at the time that this knife exists (in fact, I’ll go make this clearer by adding a timestamp somewhere but this knife doesn’t even show anomalous properties until like 100+ years into Omega-K), they don’t believe there’s any chance they can bring normal death back when they do their redefinition.

And now comes the part where I play heavily into my Foundation Headcanon. The key point in Violet’s letter states that

The world has forced us to redefine the status quo, but it is not our job to change it back.

This plays into a version of the Foundation who puts the containment of the anomalous, and maintaining normalcy above all else. Since they redefined normalcy a hundred years ago, they means they’ve changed their stance on how they will be dealing with the omega-k crisis. This version of the Foundation would see using 4514 as akin to letting civilians use that anomalous coffee machine to make medicine (iirc that’s how that anomaly works). Sure, it would help people, but it’s using an anomalous object on civilians with is largely against the rules.

That book is written assuming they are trying to restore normalcy from the way it was been changed. However, now normalcy is defined differently, and new methods for dealing with Omega-K have been devised. That excerpt is technically out of date.

Yes, I do understand that like, this is a lot of reading into the exact contexts of when the excerpt is created, and understanding when 4514 takes place in the time line (I'm going to add a time stamp to clear that up), but I felt like adding too much to the justification would make the piece a slog, even though I do have a particular line of reasoning.

Also, as more of a side-note, the Foundation would need to stab about 150k people a day to keep up with the current death rate. Even if they pull the trick where they melt the dagger down into bullets, you still don’t even have that many bullets. Using this one thing to reestablish the old death count numbers would be a nightmare, and most likely unfeasible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See, I like this, but there's one thing that bugs me; on the EoD hub, the Foundation defines the following to be one of the three main priorities for dealing with ΩK.

3. Research Terminal State Replacement: If true death is not achievable, an alternative must be engineered for ethical reasons. It has been deemed unacceptable to let mankind suffer indefinitely due to decaying biological components.

I get why you have the 'actually no we won't use it' twist here, but it just seems, like, unbelievably stupid on their part? They could offer a 'new treatment' that can end life, take people into a back room, and stab them — the veil remains, nobody's suspicious, and priority 3 is (at least partially) fulfilled.

I like the article, but I'm downvoting because I honestly cannot see the Foundation's internal logic here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
:(

Well-played.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Real good stuff, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What patooie said. I was also going to downvote due to the fact that isn't any more anomalous than an object from the Anomalous Objects list. I was pleasantly surprised.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you enjoyed!

I was originally going to downvote due to the similarity to the shard of glass that kills you

I actually talked to CWW shortly after this went up when they pointed out the similarities as well. I uh… kind of forgot that other piece had existed when I wrote this, but luckily the twists are different enough, even if the core concept shares similarities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was originally going to downvote due to the similarity to the shard of glass that kills you, but the ending puts this SCP in a context that it can function incredibly well in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm upvoting for the clever concept, but that last line is just narmy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonderfully executed twist and lead-in. End of Death was my favorite out of the Doomsday contest, and this has me pretty hyped for more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this but I ruined the reveal for myself before reading the whole thing because of that flashing text. Beyond the fact that it's super annoying, I was drawn to click it before reading the rest of the article and then saw the link to the end of death thing, so I knew what was going on from the get go. Obviously not your fault, but I really dislike that flashing text.

Beyond that though, this is a pretty clever entry into the end of death series. I found some of the wording in the O5 note to be a little awkward (and fairly informal), but I did like the "it's not our job to change it back line". Overall, +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like Scantron's shard of glass that kills you, but in the other direction. What the fuck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been missing simple Foundation objects for awhile now, and this fills my desire quite nicely.

SEASON TWO H Y P E

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty slick - at first the reader suspects that there's some kind of cognitohazard in play affecting the Foundation in-universe, and then the message at the end (which neatly manages to avoid being an explanatory note thanks to its in-universe context) provides a nice twist ending that completely recontextualizes the piece. A good example of crosslinking and hopefully a sign of things to come.

item.title: WE'RE BACK BABY
item.content:encoded:
It's been what? Like 3 months or so? Well… hopefully this will be worth the wait. Season 2 of End of Death HERE WE COME!

Also, cool trivia thing, if you convert each digit of 4514 to a letter based off of its position in the alphabet, you'd get 4 = D, 5 = E, 1 = A, 4 = D. So really this is SCP-DEAD. :P

I'd like to thank all my critiquers: UraniumEmpire, DrChandra, Veieidaheimer, Croquembouche, Othellothecat, A Random Day, Trennderdios, and AnnRKiss.

Also the code for that little HTML collapsible is stolen from Kalinin's SCP-2888, so thanks for that!

Update: ok I ended up scrapping kal's code for a sexier version Croquembouche wrote, so thanks to both of you for the idea and implementation respectively.

Here's to the fear of forever. This is just the beginning, and the near is not nigh.

Image Credit
– hide block
